description: Thursday 19 February 2026 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment and Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025 Hearing of evidence Submitters List: Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade Vangelis Vitalis, Deputy Secretary, Trade and Economic Group Deighton Condor, Unit Manager, Strategy and Performance Division Geoff King, Chief Economist/Head of Research and Analysis Unit, Economic Division James Messent, Unit Manager, Trade Policy, Engagement and Implementation Division Ministry of Business, Employment and Innovation Nic Blakeley, Chief Executive Paul Stocks, Deputy Secretary, Building Resource Markets Robyn Henderson, General Manager, Economic Growth Hamish Wilson, Manager, Economic Strategy and Insights Katherine Silvester, Principal Adviser, Economic Strategy and Insights
descriptionSummary: Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment and Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025. Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade. Vangelis Vitalis, Deputy Secretary, Trade and Economic Group

chunks.text: If we wanna do one Yeah. Bit paper then Yeah. It's this. If anyone wants to pop it, just sending paper. Yeah. Just write, write it down and I'll get this on. It's on it. Way good. Right. Um, for my thanks for joining us, we have got 45 minutes, minutes, uh, set aside for this, uh, hearing into the long term insights briefing.

So I'll let you, um, some of you obviously were know committee. I'll let you introduce yourselves and then if you wanna make some, uh, brief opening remarks and we'll, we'll open up questions. Great. I'm, uh, thank you for having us this morning. Um, my name's, uh, Nick Blakely. I'm the, uh, CE of mb it of about four weeks.

So, um, pleased to be in the role. And I'm joined here by, um, Paul Stocks, uh, deputy secretary from mb, um, and Van Go, fat, deputy secretary at mfa and Jeff King, chief, um, at mfa. And we've got, uh, some of our other colleagues, um, here as well, um, depending on how, how, how difficult your questions are and when we need to, uh, draw on, draw on them as well.

Um, so, uh, this is a joint, um, long-term insights briefing between our two agencies. We're really pleased to be here this morning to, to talk to you about it. Um, it, uh, recognizes the, the combination of productivity is the long term economics challenge, and also the fact that we are in a, an international environment where we, we must export, um, for our success.

And so bringing in that international perspective, um, we went through a process here around literature review, analysis, um, consultations, round the consultation, um, to come up with, um, uh, here, if I just sort of pull out some of the main, um, points that we can then, um, dig into more, in more depth.

Um, you know, first, uh, productivity growth is at the heart of, of future prosper disparity. That's, um, no surprise to to any of you, but the New Zealand story hasn't been very good, uh, over the last, um, uh, couple of decades or so, um, as we've been growing further behind. And in fact, if anything, our export mix has been getting less complex rather than more, which is what you'd hope.

So then lifting productivity will require moving into those higher growth, higher value areas. But we're in a world of, uh, global challenges with the geopolitical shifts that we're now seeing, um, technological change, environmental pressures, demographic change. We've gotta think about our policy making in that context.

Um, and one of the core points in the briefing is that, um, governments can have a role in both horizontal policies that is policies that affect the whole economy and all sectors of the economy. And then also, uh, vertical policies where you're looking at a particular area or a particular sector. Um, and, um, our argument in the briefing is that you've still gotta do the horizontal policies.

So as a critical to get right and continue to improve. Uh, and then as well as that there is a benefit to looking at some of those vertical areas of the, um, of policy making and, and where argument is that, uh, we haven't been very consistent, uh, and, um, deliberate over time in New Zealand about how we've done that.

Um, international connections and trade is one way of overcoming some of those, uh, natural, um, disadvantages that New Zealand has in terms the size and distance. Uh, and you know, in the briefing we talk about what might that, um, trade environment look like for New Zealand in future? What are some potential scenarios?

And I know Van go will be, will be very keen to talk about that. Um, and then we end up in the briefing with a number of, um, principles for how we think, um, uh, it's good to, to make these sort of decisions, taking a long term perspective, being deliberate about and where the focus is. Um, and using that range of government policy levers, um, to, uh, bring about change.

So we are now obviously using these, um, insights in our policy making, but really keen to share, um, the, the thinking in more detail, um, and really have to open up to questions. Thank you, your Honor. Thanks very much. Um, just to make a few observations sort of from the international trade policy side, um, I wanna make three observations.

The first on the relationship between trading productivity, the second on that international environment. 'cause as we were thinking about the long-term insights briefing, we kind of wanted to factor in what does that look like? And then third, to really focus in on one of the particular case studies that we have in the, um, in the report, which is on the single current market and what its future might look like, uh, the Australian New Zealand one.

So the relationship between, as, as Nick has said, there is a strong relationship between trade and productivity and, um, work that the ministry has done. So the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and trade, uh, in a working paper underlines how important that connection is. So we know that, um, and exporting firm, uh, that exports goods has an 89% higher output per worker than a non-sporting, uh, company.

That productivity gap is even greater if you're a large firm. So if you're a large firm of 250 plus and you're exporting your productivity gap compared to a non-sport domestic is 130%. So these are significant. Uh, there, unfortunately, one of the challenges is that we are one of the most weakly connected, um, OECD economies.

Uh, and the OCD says that up to half or at least half of our poor productivity performs can be accounted for by this weak international, um, connectivity that we have. And then sort of if you think about that connectivity, um, you then have to think about what the international kind of trade policy outlook look like.

And I know I've spoken, uh, before to this committee about that. Suffice to say, and to use the Canadian Prime Minister's term, we are facing a rupture. Uh, there's disorder, there's volatility, there's increased uncertainty. And we unfortunately don't see that changing over the period of the long insights briefing.

In fact, some of that may be exacerbated by, uh, events largely out of, uh, new Zealand's control. Um, but I guess what the, the point we look to make in the insights briefing is that we do have agency here. So we are a small state in a world of disorder, uh, and a state for which the golden weather for New Zealand trade policy really is over.

But we do have the ability to kind of manage some of these big risks that are out there. And that management process includes the free trade agreements that we've been negotiating. Um, so one of the factoids that we have in here is that more than 70% of our exports, uh, are now covered by, uh, not the World Trade Organization, but actually these free trade agreements that are in place that are international treaties that give us, uh, that scope.

But one thing to say about those free trade agreements, uh, the great that they are, they are removing barriers at the border. The more interesting thing that we need to be thinking about is what can we do about the barriers behind the border? Because the big productivity gains are the dynamic productivity gains we know from OCD research is by dealing with the behind the border, uh, barriers.

Once of course, you've got rid of the more obvious barriers like tariffs and so on. So that, that brought us then to start thinking about, uh, how do we think about the deepening integration of our economy into the region, but also particularly with Australia. And we have a terrific existing project, um, with Australia.

Sometimes we forget that the origins of that was a 1966 New Zealand Australia free trade agreement. Uh, we would look back on that agreement and criticize it as a poor quality, low, uh, coverage agreement. But over time, that became the foundation stone for, uh, a key part of our, uh, economy and certainly a core part of our export focus.

Of course, that's the CER, which is of course morphed into the single economic market as we described in the, in the report. And perhaps there are three elements in the single economic market that make it really interesting. There's the freedom movement of people, of course, there's also the Trans Tasman Mutual recognition arrangement, which of course focuses on qualifications, but also on standards for, for goods.

Uh, and the third area is the very interesting work, um, led out of other agencies including, uh, colleagues from MB on business law, competition, policy coordination, and where possible, uh, looking to align those. So the question we had as we were thinking about, you know, what's our future here is what's the future of the singling home market?

We have kind of, to use a well-worn phrase, we've, we've squeezed a lot of juice outta that lemon. There is definitely more juice to be squeezed out of it. So there are things we can still do in that space that we deliver benefit both for Australia and for New Zealand. But given the international environment and the challenges we face the sorter out there, uh, and the challenges we face in some of our key markets, is it time to think about is the future of the single economic market only a determined bilateral is?

Or is it actually about moving it out into the wider region and thinking about our place in that region? And the area that we suggest we need to be thinking about here is the Ian, the 10 Ian countries they account, they will account for nearly 10% of the global population. Um, they're the, a very dynamic and fast growing one.

And they also have their own regional economic integration project, the Ian Regional economic community. So the question we ask is, how do we better align ourselves as a single economic market, Australia and New Zealand with the 10 economies as they develop their own trajectory of regional economic integration?

And that brought us to think about how could this work in practice? And we suggest some ways in which we might want to think about that over the coming years in terms of a kind of a variable geometry approach. It's clear we're not talking about integrating market into Ian, uh, that that is a very long distant, uh, prospect for, for a range of reasons.

But are there ways that we could more coherently align ourselves? Where would we start? Uh, and what we suggest is perhaps one way to start is to think about the fact that we already have a free trade agreement, Australia and New Zealand with Ian, it's the Ian Australia New Zealand free trade agreement. So the key barriers at the border are already gone.

99% of our trade is tariff free, uh, into there. What more do we need to be doing, and who would we start with? And there we argue in the report that a place to start would be with Singapore. So are there elements of our existing s market structure that we could work with Singapore on?

Are there practical areas where there's benefit for Singapore, benefit for New Zealand, a benefit for Australia to deepen our integration. Both of us have enhanced strategic partnership agreements, uh, both Australia and New Zealand. With Singapore, we've got all the institutional structures we need, but do we want to think about that in a more, uh, creative and future focused, uh, areas?

So that was one of the, the key areas we, we po for, uh, for us to be thinking about into the future. Okay. We'll, uh, start with, uh, Han. I Thank you Mr. Chair. I thank you Tim. Jordan, congratulations. It's good to see you. Um, the report notes a number of matters about where we might shift to Singapore, you, me, um, the other one is defense, and in particular the way that we look towards technologies in this space.

Um, a concern that I had in reading that was just how far behind we might appear to be at least, uh, on the global stage with respect to not only defense spending, but the kind of technologies that people are exploring that comes to defense. I wondered if you could just share a little bit of insight or view into why.

I mean, it's, it's in the report here. Um, and I know that just last year there was the, uh, defense, um, industry strategic plan, which I think is a very good thing. Um, but to my reading, that's something definitely for a little bit further down the line. But you highlighted in here, I wondered if you had any further reflections on that.

Is it, lemme make a few, um, few, uh, observations. So o overall, in terms of the structure of our economy, yes, we're still very dominated by, um, primary, um, production, primary sector. That's our natural strength. Um, what you've seen from, uh, another small advanced economies is that they maintain that and they build on that.

And then, um, over time, new other sectors emerge. And, and that's what we haven't seen as much of in the New Zealand economy. So that's one of the, the challenges. Um, often those sectors will be adjacent in some sense to those existing sectors, whether, um, so you with, uh, primary you'd expect, um, agritech and, and things, bio manufacturing, things close to those to emerge.

And then of course there are other sectors that start to emerge as well. And the New Zealand's, uh, space aerospace, um, area has been one of those that, um, you know, was a bit of a shot out of the blue. You know, it came, you know, really on the back of, um, Beck's work. But there is, it's not just Rocket Lab. There is a quite a large, um, nexus of companies now emerging.

And so that is an area where, um, we, we are developing capability. And so, you know, in our, in terms of our horizontal and vertical question is, well, what are some of those vertical, um, levers that government could use that could further support those areas. Um, clearly there are, where there might be regulatory, um, barriers, that's, um, that's something to be considering.

Um, but in the case of, you know, defense tech, those other levers could be things like, um, science policy and, and what is the, the science that's going into that area. It could be, um, procurement and defense thinking about, um, what are the types of capabilities it needs and where would it look to procure those. Um, I think defense is now working on a, a tech accelerator, um, where, um, exploring whether that could be closely working with the New Zealand Institute of Advanced Technology so that we can use, again, some

of these other sorts of levers to help accelerate development in those sectors. So those are some of the things where we think this thinking takes us in terms of government's Response. Excellent. Janet? Yeah, I, I was sort of interested in, um, a bit more about your strategic choice of Singapore as place for potential integration.

Um, you know, what other, what other kind of countries were you looking at? Uh, I think Singapore, there's some obvious things, you know, relative size, uh, and that kind of thing, but I was just wonder to know a little bit more and also, um, what you think that might look like if we done that track.

Yeah, that's, it's a, it's a great Question. Um, and I think it was driven, um, in large part by the existence of a institutional structures that we have already that exist, not just between Australia and New Zealand, but also with Singapore that we don't have with, uh, with many other partners. So the, the fact of the series of trade agreements we have with Singapore, which have increasingly deepened the integration with us, the fact that during COVID we developed that supply chains and post COVID series of supply chains agreement

that more tightly integrate us, uh, with Singapore. And then, uh, we also were trying to think of, uh, how would you engage with our Ian colleagues and what is the best way to do that in a way that's both efficient and effective and also would drive productivity back for, for us?

So while there are clearly larger economies in, uh, in the Ian group, um, working with Singapore as your starting point is, is an effective way because, uh, we, we know each other's systems well. Uh, the existing institutional structure, so the strategic partnership agreements that both we in Australia have, have deepened the integration.

You asked what kinds of things practically. So for example, you could see a situation where, um, you'd use the trans Tasman mutual recognition arrangement as a model for the way in which you'd approach, you know, what kind of standards does Singapore have on agricultural products? What kind of standards do we have on manufacturing products?

Is there a productivity dividend from saying, look, we recognize yours, uh, so we wouldn't have to develop our own, that requires a lot of trust and relationship building and so on. And as we looked across the 10, uh, Singapore clearly, uh, emerges as a, as a terrific place to start.

I think the interesting question then becomes, how much can you do with Singapore? How does that influence the da the trajectory of what Ian is also trying to do? So Ana is the, the Ian Regional economic community is very determined not to be an EU project, EU style project. So they're not willing to give up sovereignty.

So what kind of economic integration are they thinking about? And what we think is that the way in which we've done that with Australia without, by preserving our sovereignty, but having these shared arrangements, that that might be a model that asan might find, uh, useful as well. And proof of concept would be through the work that we were able to do with, um, with Singapore.

So you can see, for example, the work we might do in the green technology space. Um, uh, you know, if you're filing, uh, your securities offerings in one country, could you file it in all three? That, those kinds of things that would be practical benefits for business, uh, quickly are other kinds of things that we're driving our, our thinking there.

But it is really about also trying to make sure that, uh, the relevance, the economic relevance of New Zealand continues to rise and be amplified by the engagement with our as young colleagues. Uh, I think they're important lessons to be learned from the way that Swiss, the Norwegians, Lichtenstein and Iceland and the European free trade area have tried to align themselves, not integrate, but align themselves with, uh, the European Union and looking to have influenced themselves on that emerging structure to protect their own national interest.

Yeah. Um, just just a follow up question. Um, was the way that they, uh, sort of navigate relationship with China, did that factor into your thinking? I matter? Yeah, yeah. No, no, it's, it's, it's a fair question. Um, in, in this case, no, that wasn't a, a driver.

We were looking at the, at the economics of this. Okay. Um, rather than the geopolitics, um, the geopolitics is certainly a, an element that we're always having to think about, particularly given the behavior that we've seen recently from, from major economies. Uh, so that sense, the driver has been, yes, there is a geopolitical element, but not so much the Singapore China piece, but more generally and the need for a small state like ours to build its own resilience.

So our, our strategy in the trade world has been three pass rules, uh, resilience and innovation. And so that resilience piece is all about kind of positioning the economy to kind of manage and mitigate some of that turbulence that's out there. And we see the, the deepening of the single joint market and the, uh, expansion out through a variable geometry approach as one way to, to tackle that. Um, why this, Um, I was kind of shocked to see a, um, chart recently, which talked about if there was an invasion of ti of Taiwan,

just how economically disruptive that was. I had underestimated that in these plans, how I, I totally appreciate what you're saying, but in terms of keeping things in different baskets, I think we've learned that it's so unstable out there, we need a lot of baskets.

So I appreciate picking a region, but how have you balanced that, having different areas? So if one is disrupted, we have other areas which are safer. I mean, from a general economic point of view, you might talk on the, the countryside, but I think your point about diversification is, uh, is a good one.

And we would agree, um, with that. And when you have a look at our, um, export mix, whether that's on sectors or on, um, uh, countries, and that we ex export to, uh, we're still very concentrated, uh, in primary production and in private production To, To China. Um, and so for all sorts of reasons, um, uh, that, um, different parts of the world would be growing at different times, it's good to have diversification across your economy that that gets you resilience.

So we would agree with the point, and that's partly why, um, and seeing the economy develop in ways that develop out these different sectors over time, we think is a positive, um, for productivity, but also because it builds in. So that's a general, um, development of the economy. But do you wanna talk about Yeah, I mean, this is very much a, a focus of ours and, and I think the way to see what I talked about terms, the single income market is part of a wider strategy.

Um, so this creation of optionality, uh, or diversification as like I said, has been a really important driver of what we've been trying to do. Uh, so there's been a long history of New Zealand being over concentrated in particular markets, in particular products, going back to the relationship with the British. Yeah. Um, important lessons learned from that.

And now we're in another world where, um, some of our longstanding friends and partners aren't behaving the way we would like them to, and so we're having to kind of create more optionality there. And that's been, so if you think about less than 10 years ago, uh, about 40 to 50% of our exports were covered by trade agreement.

We've now lifted that. And part of that has been designed to say to businesses, um, China is a really important market, uh, and you've got tariff preferences, in other words, advantages over your competitors in there because of the free trade agreement. But now for the first time, you can have another look at the eu, you can have another look at the UK where we have not had advantages.

We have not had a level, but we do now. And we're saying to business, you need to have a look at that as well. And I think the recently concluded, India free trade agreement is in a similar kind of area where we're trying to create optionality. So another large market middle class in India will be 500 million people that's larger than the eu. It's larger than the tenian countries.

Here's a, a a place where you might be able to compete now for the first time, again on a level playing, but it's about creating that optionality. But it's not just about negotiating agreements, it's also about the implementation of them and making sure that businesses take advantage, full advantage of them, that they're cited on the opportunity and that they could utilize them, uh, as much as they can.

So a big part of the work that we do across agencies, not just, uh, in fact, but N-P-I-N-B, uh, customs and so on, is to help business access those other opportunities to help them diversify, uh, and manage their risk. Um, Laura Cle, Um, thank you. You kind of started answering my question. Um, to supplement from that, I am out there talking to businesses all the time, um, and I think there is like an, a empathy or a, a view, um, that is different to ours.

I don't think the risk is so apparent to our businesses. Um, are you guys doing any work, like whether it's with NZTE, um, to talk to, um, exporters and then about, um, you know, their market resilience and the geopolitical risks and how to make sure that they, whether that's things coming in, um, to, man, I know like a lot of imports come in from certain places to be able to manufacture here in New Zealand Zealand or exporting out.

Um, and when I talk to 'em about it, they're always like, oh, yeah, like really surprised or like, haven't really thought about that. And these, some of these like, are big exporters. So, um, is there any work in that space to, um, sort of create that kind of like resilience? I mean, obviously we promote the other trade agreements, but you know, the messaging around why that is so important, and I think Yeah. But maybe to make three, three points.

I mean, it is concerning to hear that, um, my own impression is that, um, businesses are realizing that the world has really fundamentally changed. There have been a series of shocks, sorry. Oh, no, no, no. After you finish. Um, there have been a series of shocks to our system, um, not just the United States, but we mustn't forget that the way China recently dealt with Australia was a reminder to us that the big guys really are playing outside the rules, uh, and that that really is painful and risk.

And so we saw what happened to Australia, uh, we saw what happened to their crayfish exports, to their coal exports, uh, to their wine exports. I mean, they've literally stopped over 48 hours. Uh, and that did not resume, uh, for nearly 18 months to two years, depending on the product we're talking about. So the message to business has been, look at what's happening, and you do need to be thinking about that.

So there have been a series of sort of public engagements and ministerial speeches, uh, presentations that we've been making, and we've looked to present that ft a strategy in that vein of trying to create those optionality. Look, the second area that I encourage people to look at is, um, well, thanks to Jeff's scene, we, we produce market intelligence reports.

Mm-hmm. And those market intelligence reports highlight, uh, first of all the rising geopolitical risk and the challenge for New Zealand exporters, but they also try in a very practical way to identify opportunities for those exporters, uh, as they, as they go out. So the, the management of that risk is very important.

I, I mean, you, you know, when you meet New Zealand companies, often they, their real focus is meeting payroll next week, the challenges that they, that they have. But at the same time, as we've seen with the United States, when President Trump raised the tariff, uh, know effectively from less than 2% on New Zealand to 10 and then to 15, that has been a brutal shock to a whole lot of businesses that thought, yeah, nothing to worry about here.

We've got certainty into the US market. Uh, that was a reminder, I think, that there is no certainty in this world now. And that combination, I think I, I'd like to think, is forcing businesses to think really about that. And what we are trying to do as, as government, is to kind of find ways to support them both in the implementation of the FDA's highlighting the risk, the market intelligence reporting that that's free, you know?

Mm-hmm. Two businesses on our, on our websites. So regular, so readable format's, not, not our usual in fact contribution. Nice. Google that. I'm gonna send people your way. Help. Can I just, um, add, um, the, yeah. There's no doubt that New Zealand's benefited hugely from the, the rules based, um, system. Mm-hmm. Um, uh, and obviously it's going to be brutal in, uh, in, in, uh, AL'S words.

Um, but we have to think about what are the opportunities in that for New Zealand. So from an economic security lens, a lot more focus on economic security, um, for countries around the world. And there are things that New Zealand produces that other countries are very interested in. Obviously food has always been one of those, and, and, and we're very good at making, uh, uh, food, but they're increasingly, you talked about defense technologies.

S some is, is, um, exporting into the, into the uk. Um, you've got, uh, areas of critical minerals, which I know has been in the, in, in the vape recently. Those are areas where the rest of the world is looking for what could New Zealand play in that. So we'd encourage looking at what could be the opportunities in this new world, even though, um, you know, it won't be as, uh, as plain sailing as the previous world. Mm-hmm.

Okay. We've so got 15 minutes. I'm tracking Tiana, Melissa in Pny. So are they sub new questions? Sort Of, sort of sub Yeah, I think everyone sort of sucks. Okay. Um, 'cause you talked before in response to us, uh, one of the questions about sort of some of those countries, Stein on the side of the EU and how they're aligning. But in a, in the Finland example, I think it was, you talked about how they were sort of on the fringe, uh, geographically, and so they'd moved from commodity to knowledge. Um, we, we we're obviously far more on the fringe geographically, and that offers some strength in terms of a changing world in terms of security and domestic policy.

But when it comes to international parade, that's a, perhaps that strength becomes a challenge. Um, and I'm just wondering where you see the opportunities, if we think long term Denmark example had sort of the triple helix with the investment into academia. Singapore, you also talked about growing that knowledge base.

Is that an area we need to be looking at? We, you know, we can't live off commodities, um, even high value ones like crayfish, but, you know, dairy and protein, we can't live off that forever. Where is the opportunity for New Zealand? How do we exploit the advantages we have without them becoming our greatest weakness? I can start there as well.

Um, I, I think the, you know, our main point here is that the way economies evolve is to look for those adjacencies. So it will be some of those sectors that builds on our traditional strengths, but then, um, uses technology to look for new areas. And that is your agritech, um, your biomanufacturing. Um, uh, you know, at biomedical we have very strong, um, uh, you know, health technologies emerging sector as well.

So it's looking for where are those areas of New Zealand's strength, and then how could, uh, a combination of, um, government policy levers help support, um, an acceleration of those. So, you know, we talk about obviously space in advanced, um, aviation here, but it is about trying to look for those opportunities and, you know, the case studies that is what those other small advanced economies have done, many of those islands, Finland, built on primary, um, sector, and then have developed those and also, um, found new sectors to evolve as well.

Yeah, there's a degree of path to PC here. Um, and I suppose the old saying is, keep on doing what you did, you'll get the same thing. So, uh, our primary sectors didn't evolve by accident. We put a lot of support into them over many years, uh, through the science system, through the way that we've approached our trade agreements.

Uh, and so what started as a natural advantage, we actually built on, and, you know, put, as I say, all of our research and, and investment around that, we put our trade diplomats to make sure we had free trade agreements. That's brilliant. And we are operating really, really well there. But we do need to think about, uh, what kind of support like that would we put around our emerging industries.

So as Nick says, there are adjacencies like agritech, you'd think about, well, in the advanced managed capturing space, uh, how do you use our public research organizations to, to work on that, uh, in the areas of, you know, um, it actually, new Zealand's had quite a large, uh, growth in services, uh, and services is an area where, uh, people transport notwithstanding distance doesn't make such a huge impact.

So, uh, we, we are focusing as well as the horizontal lever as well as market integrations. Uh, and as a side issue there, um, one of the things I'm responsible for is competition policy. Uh, and our view largely is it's very hard to drive a thriving competition in a country of 5 million people. But if you can think about conceptually expanding your borders, the ECM to the Australian markets, to, to Singapore, et cetera, and having the systems and standards in place that look very, very similar, then actually you've essentially expanded your domestic

markets to 30, 40, 50 million people kind of overnight.
